item.title: Values to standards
item.dc:creator: mcr

item.title: Professional development advice from Tom, Kylie, Drake and Kimmie
item.dc:creator: mcr
item.content:encoded:
So, you've made it past your first twelve months. You went into meetings, took notes, worked hard, had good ideas and every now and then presented your work to the rest of the team. You've done well and should be proud of yourself. You are now one of the crew, no longer a young whippersnapper but a seasoned and competent contributor. Now you're wondering: Is that it? What's next? When and where do I get promoted? The short answer is: not so fast. This is not the German military service, where three months without shooting your comrades will get you the first promotion . However, there is hope and I'm happy to share my perspective on how you might end up where you want to be. It's a simple process – four steps, nine words, no hashtags:
  1. Set goals
  2. Reflect
  3. Get feedback
  4. Love what you do
Let's start with the first step: set goals. Remember Top Gun? Of course you do. If not, stop here, go stream/ rent/ borrow the movie and come back. This is essential viewing. In this 1980s classic Tom Cruise plays the role of Pete Mitchell a.k.a Maverick, a young F14 pilot. He is given the chance to train at the Navy's Fighter Weapons School at Miramar in San Diego. Having barely made it into the academy, the first thing he does, is declaring that he wants to win the trophy for best pilot at the end of the academy. While that might have been an overconfident move, he did the right thing: setting himself a goal. Something that he can align his actions towards. Spoiler alert: In the end, he doesn't achieve his goal, but that's not the point. His goal helped him move forward with intent and purpose rather than cruising along (no pun intended). The same can be applied to your job and I encourage everyone to set themselves two kinds of goals: (1) contribution goals and (2) personal development goals. Contribution goals are the achievements, the results that you are going to produce. Those individual goals should directly contribute to the larger goals of your organisation. If not, this might be a good time to discuss your goals with your manager. She should help you understand the bigger picture and make sure that you set yourself meaningful goals. A PD plan is very much a mental and written commitment to yourself Take it seriously, like any other project – with care and with pride Schedule some time with yourself to write out your plan (and be generous with time), slow down and think Don’t jump the gun and go straight into My Future and start typing everything in your head → Make sure that you are clear on both. For the business objectives, spend enough time to clarify them. The clearer they are for you and your manager, the better the chance that you will do the right thing to hit them. Also: clarify the why. This is important. A sense of purpose and connecting to it can be a powerful motivator. Find that for yourself. Don't wait for your manager to come up with it. This is your job. Once you have that sense of purpose, it will be very gratifying. For the personal development: this are things you want to get better at. Think about where you want to develop and what you need for that Don't pick too many, but rather focus on one or two Take the time to think about: what are the 3-5 experiences that I need to really improve on each. Goal: Lofty and aspirational → Should get you going Experiences: Concrete and actionable → Clearly identifiable action items that will get you there That doesn't mean necessarily going to courses, but could also mean getting dedicated work assignments. It will help you manager paying attention throughout the year and be your ally on looking out for you. Share those goals. There is no shame about having development goals. What's the worst that can happen: somebody gives you bad advice, the best: somebody gives you great advice Tell the story about Frank Tell your performance goal: Getting better at public speaking Coursera Ignite Improv classes Present at least once a month Google has its form of performance management OKRs published for everyone to see. Helps figuring out what each others' priorities are. Supports collaboration. Exercise: Write down one development goal that you want to work on between now and the end of the financial year Figure out two or three things that you want to do to get to that goal

item.title: Software people
item.dc:creator: mcr
item.content:encoded: Software is often seen in the context of an app, a full-blown application suite or a website. Traditionally it was easy to identify software. Before the 1970s software was limited to a set of numerical problems like calculating flight trajectories, developed using punch cards. With the 1970s came keyboards, software increased its scope to textual problems like word processing and accounting. And in the 1990s, it was hard to find a computer flyer that didn't praise the multimedia of its products. Computers could accept all kinds of input: visual, acoustic, text, you name it. Nowadays it goes even further. Standard smartphones have so many sensors and are so well connected with their environment that software can basically typically in the context of making things easier or automating processes. It is a feature or a product and in traditional organisations often an afterthought. However, while software always will involve some form of code, it is more a lifestyle than a feature.

item.title: Leadership economics – of fishes and ponds
item.dc:creator: mcr
item.content:encoded:
Relatively spoken, would you rather be a big fish in a small pond or a small fish in a big pond? Or asked differently: Would you rather have 1% market share of a $10B market or 40% market share of a $250m market. The instinctive answer is, that it doesn't matter, because in both cases you're a $100m business and that is a pretty sweet deal. An answer that I've heard a lot of startups pitch is that they'd rather go for the big market, because then even a small share of that market gives you a sizeable business. Since you are so small, you probably fly under the radar of the big fishes and even better, there is this huge market that gives you potential to grow even further. The opportunity is limitless.
This all sounds reasonable, especially when you're of the ambitious kind that thinks about the growth potential of a big market. However, one has to think about the competition. Because if you're not the big fish, chances are that someone else is. That is not very encouraging, but that's how it is.

item.title: We need to talk about meetings
item.dc:creator: mcr

item.title: On productivity
item.dc:creator: mcr
item.content:encoded: The Guardian has an excellent podcast series, where they read out some of their long reads. What a great idea! I most recently listened to the audio version of why time management is ruining our lives. The underlying question was whether we are optimising ourselves into oblivion and thereby missing the point. Since personal heroes of mine such as Merlin Mann and David Allen feature, I was naturally intruiged. Having read David Allen's Getting Things Done (GTD) multiple times and finding comfort in Inbox Zero, I'm writing these lines with a clear bias towards the productivity camp.  I can very much relate to the author's main message of "optimising your life for efficiency will ultimately not lead to happiness. Efficiency is only a small part of productivity [TK author name] uses the terms productivity and efficiency interchangably and leaves out  The perfect system fallacy (A) There is no perfect system, so stop tweaking, (B) There are diminishing rates of return in terms of getting to a complete system. There are no shortcuts Purpose drives everything

item.title: Dream jobs: Comedians in cars getting coffee
item.dc:creator: mcr
item.content:encoded: When I started my current job, one messages from the intro session stuck with me: "Crap jobs are created by other people, dream jobs you make yourself". It's a quote from Hugh MacLeod's Gaping Void. It became blatantly obvious what it means when I discovered recently Jerry Seinfeld's Comedians in cars getting coffee. Here's a guy who loves comedy, loves talking about it with fellow comedians, loves cars and appreciates a good cup of coffee. As simple as it seem, as genius it is. It's a show that probably only a handful of people on the planet can do credibly and as well as Jerry Seinfeld does it. He just took some of the things he's very passionate about and created a show around it. It's hiding in plain sight in the title. What would your show or career be called?

item.title: Having figured it out
item.dc:creator: mcr
item.content:encoded: I've got a job at one of the biggest companies in one of the fastest growing sectors. My title sounds fancy and the results of my work have resulted in large amounts of money changing hands. Do I feel like I have figured it out? Not at all. Are there lessons I can share? Most definitely. Is this uncomfortable? Oh, yes!

item.title: Why Time Flies
item.dc:creator: mcr
item.content:encoded:
A remarkable visual explanation of what we increasingly become aware of as we grow older: the years Pass increasingly faster.
An Interactive Explaination Why Time Flies by @maxkiener
Source: Why Time Flies

item.title: Using OneNote to replace your Moleskine
item.dc:creator: mcr
item.content:encoded:
For the longest time I've been carrying a physical notebook around with my to take notes with pen and paper. There is something about putting a pen on paper and taking notes or writing down your thoughts that I find very enjoyable and that helps me think. My system:
  1. One note for each day
  2. Use headers to separate sections that are collapsible
  3. Separate notes for Print-outs and scribbles
  4. Tag notes and sections within notes
  5. Use search and "recent notes"
  6. Learn the keyboard shortcuts

item.title: Rocks and Pebbles
item.dc:creator: mcr
item.content:encoded:
Writing is hard, writing an executive summary is not an exception. However, it follows certain rules that make it easier to get it right. You need to get to the point fast, need to make hard editorial decisions where to cut and distinguish the critical few statements from the comprehensive many. One analogy that always helps is that of rocks and pebbles.

Imagine a river that you have to get your audience across. To get from one side to the next without drowning you need to identify the four to five rocks they can jump on. Each rock is supported by a few pebbles that make it safe to land on.

item.title: I love a good rabbit hole
item.dc:creator: mcr
item.content:encoded:
Who doesn't like a good rabbit hole? One where you have a thought, look it up on the Wikipedia or YouTube and find yourself two hours later in the depths of some obscure pop genre ... and you don't even feel sorry for it. This is different from just following whatever YouTube's algorithm decides to show you. This is a lot more active and a lot more satisfying.

I found myself in one of those rabbit holes last night. Somehow I was reminded of an acoustic version of Sheryl Crow's "Home", looked it up on YouTube (unfortunately the only place I found it available), checked out the official video, was mesmerized by the pictures, looked up the director, Samuel Bayer, via Wikipedia and suddenly found myself watching a small part of his life's work:

  • Nirvana's Smells Like Teen Spirit
  • Blind Melon's No Rain (remember the bumble bee girl?)
  • The Crainberries' Zombie
  • Doll Parts by Hole
  • Only Happy When it Rains by Garbage

And that was just the tip of the iceberg (by the way, it was interesting to see that everything was shot in 4:3 format, which just looks so unfamiliar these days). He shot videos for David Bowie, Michael Jackson, Green Day, The Strokes, ... the list goes on and on – in addition to commercials for Nike, Coca Cola, ... you get the idea. I turned off the TV and Wikipedia about two hours later, deeply satisfied, which is something that I would never say after a YouTube session that plainly follows recommendations.

My learning is, that the recommendation engines, as much as they are able to serve us candy in the form of seductive content that is optimized for watching, still are nowhere near where a consumer would like them to be. They are optimized to support the underlying business model, which is advertising.

The other insight I took to bed with me was that consumption is not the problem, it is passive consumption that leaves me with the feeling of having just eaten a bunch of empty calories. The more active I choose what to watch, read and listen to, the better the experience in general. It might not always be the most useful knowledge, but as long as it is interesting it is typically worth the time. Hence my recommendation: Go to Wikipedia, look up something random and follow the links in the article to whatever sounds interesting. Let's see where that rabbit hole takes you.

item.title: That Squiggle of the Design Process | ReVision Lab
item.dc:creator: mcr
item.content:encoded:
The Process of Design from a great height. by Damien Newman Years ago I dropped a simple illustration into a proposal to convey the design process to a client. It was meant to illustrate the charac…

Source: That Squiggle of the Design Process | ReVision Lab

item.title: The pyramid principle
item.dc:creator: mcr
item.content:encoded: Bed writing is a meeting machine. Book should have been a blog post, should have been a tweet, should not have been published. Meeting should have been an email, should have been a text.

item.title: Know your tools
item.dc:creator: mcr

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value: Dancing with tools
Dancing with tools

item.dc:creator: mcr

item.title: Pulse Check
item.dc:creator: mcr
item.content:encoded:
Once you reach a certain age, you get introduced to the annual physical. A quick medical check to make sure that you are reasonably healthy and identify the areas that you should better take care of. We do the same for cars, critical house equipment like furnace and chimneys. It is only reasonable to do the same for our teams. And most companies do that: an annual employee engagement survey that takes ~15 minutes to fill out. A few weeks later teams get the results and in a year, it is time to do it all over again.

That kind of system identifies that major company level movements, but for individual teams it has a few flaws:

  1. Checking in on your team health only once a year is not enough. Smaller teams don't necessarily need a formal survey. Hallway conversations provide a good barometer ...
    Recency bias
  2. The questions you ask on the company level are different from those that you would ask your team. Most teams have individual needs and there is a good case for asking specific questions.
  3. There lag between taking the survey and seeing the results is often too big for the smaller course corrections.

item.title: The Work You Do, the Person You Are
item.dc:creator: mcr
item.content:encoded:
Then one day, alone in the kitchen with my father, I let drop a few whines about the job. I gave him details, examples of what troubled me, yet although he listened intently, I saw no sympathy in his eyes. No “Oh, you poor little thing.” Perhaps he understood that what I wanted was a solution to the job, not an escape from it. In any case, he put down his cup of coffee and said, “Listen. You don’t live there. You live here. With your people. Go to work. Get your money.

That was what he said. This was what I heard:

1. Whatever the work is, do it well—not for the boss but for yourself.

2. You make the job; it doesn’t make you.

3. Your real life is with us, your family.

4. You are not the work you do; you are the person you are.

I have worked for all sorts of people since then, geniuses and morons, quick-witted and dull, bighearted and narrow. I’ve had many kinds of jobs, but since that conversation with my father I have never considered the level of labor to be the measure of myself, and I have never placed the security of a job above the value of home.

Toni Morrison – The Work You Do, the Person You Are

item.dc:creator: mcr
item.content:encoded: <h1> <a href="https://doctorow.medium.com/the-memex-method-238c71f2fb46"> The Memex Method </a> </h1> <blockquote> Writers have kept notebooks since time immemorial. The auctorial equivalent to the artist’s sketchbook is the “commonplace book,” which can contain everything from newspaper clippings to grocery lists to attempts to capture those inspirational bolts out of the blue. </blockquote> <blockquote> Writing for an audience keeps me honest. </blockquote> <blockquote> If the fragment seems significant, I’ll blog it: I’ll set out the context for why I think this seems important and then describe what it adds to the picture. </blockquote> <blockquote> For example, it’s hard to write long and prolifically without cringing at the memory of some of your own work. After all, if the point of writing is to clarify your thinking and improve your understanding, then, by definition, your older work will be more muddled. </blockquote> <blockquote> Cringing at your own memories does no one any good. On the other hand, systematically reviewing your older work to find the patterns in where you got it wrong (and right!) is hugely beneficial — it’s a useful process of introspection that makes it easier to spot and avoid your own pitfalls. </blockquote>

item.title: The Digital Workplace Is Designed to Bring You Down
item.dc:creator: mcr
item.content:encoded:
So how do you actually work with your mind and create things of value? What I’ve identified is three principles:

  • doing fewer things, 
  • working at a natural pace, but
  • obsessing over quality.

That trio of properties better hits the sweet spot of how we’re actually wired and produces valuable meaningful work, but it’s sustainable.

The Digital Workplace Is Designed to Bring You Down - The New York Times (nytimes.com)

item.title: Operating models
item.dc:creator: mcr
item.content:encoded:
You know why playing a game is fun? Because it has rules, and you have a way to win. Picture a bunch of people showing up at an athletic field with random equipment and no rules. Someone is going to get hurt. You don’t know how to play, you don’t know how to score, and you don’t know how to win.” It’s critical for companies and teams to establish the playing field on which everyone participates and marks progress.

My current job description is to develop an operating model for a larger software organization.

item.dc:creator: mcr
item.content:encoded:
He targeted large universities across the country with students from a wide range of backgrounds. Within these schools, he looked for those who had put themselves in leadership positions, be it in ROTC, sports, a fraternity or sorority, student government, or the arts, among other things, so long as they had taken ownership of something. Preferably, he wanted to see examples of resilience and persistence in these pursuits. In short, he was looking for kids who had embraced and indulged in everything the university had to offer, all while excelling academically
acknowledging that continuing to fish in a few select ponds that haven’t grown in more than three decades probably isn’t the optimal way to source talent anymore. Instead, it means stretching your hiring lens, looking in places you haven’t before, and taking chances on less conventional candidates in order to find diamonds in the rough.
https://collabfund.com/blog/find-the-savages/

item.title: Decoder guest host Hank Green makes Nilay Patel explain why websites have a future
item.dc:creator: mcr
item.content:encoded: Nilay Patel knocked it out of the park recently with two podcast episodes. In the first one he had YouTuber Hank Green interview him on Nilay's own Decoder podcast. A fascinating conversation about Complexly co-founder and YouTuber Hank Green guest hosts Decoder to interview Nilay Patel on the future of the web. — Read on www.theverge.com/24087834/hank-green-decoder-podcast-google-youtube-web-media-platforms-distribution-future

item.title: Good execution
item.dc:creator: mcr
item.content:encoded:
A few years ago, I had the honor to take the stage at Ignite Sydney. Ignite is an event where anybody can submit an idea for a topic that they want to present. The presentation format comes with two main constrains:

  • The presentation must not be longer or shorter than 5 minutes in total
  • Every slide auto-advances after 15 seconds, which gives the presenter exactly 20 slides than run in the background.

It's a great format that I can highly recommend Trying out.

I talked about what I learned about productivity by taking dance lessons. At the time I was newly married and dance lessons took me out of my comfort zone. A lot. At the same time, I got a fresh perspective on productivity systems. Productivity systems have been a guilty pleasure of mine ever since reading David Allen's Getting Things Done. One central idea of Getting Things Done is that the brain is good for having ideas, but not for holding them – let alone managing them. The other idea is that when we systematically transform ideas into "shovel-ready work", we lower the bar for good execution. David Allen describes a whole system of inboxes, lists and reference folders. In its simplest form it is about gathering input, reviewing it on a regular basis and then deciding what to do about it.

I've read the book multiple times and learn something new every time. I love the central idea of separating planning from execution.

Good execution is focused work that has a clear direction, creates a feedback loop and

Promise and deliver

Characteristics of good planning

- Transparent --> Can be audited, has clear assumptions

- Realistic: Plan for the worst and hope for the best

- Acknowledges trade offs

- Easy to understand: "Mom" is not a good label for a todo. "Call mom to ask her about gardening tips" is a much better description. This starts with the individual. Did you ever wrote down something important you had to do. And then forgot what it meant because your shorthand was too vague? Now imagine establishing a plan for a team or an entire organization. Those plans need to be easily digestible. Simple words, clear messages,

Characteristics of good execution

item.title: This is a test post
item.dc:creator: mcr
item.content:encoded: Let's see how this is going. Nothing new to be seen here. Let's just write a few words and sentences.

item.title: Documentation is rigor in the service of kindness
item.dc:creator: mcr
item.content:encoded: It's hard to get anyone excited about documentation. Writing things down is considered a chore by most.

item.title: Removing friction
item.dc:creator: mcr
item.content:encoded: After 12 years , the 2024/2025 season was the one we got back into skiing. We got

a:17:{s:2:"to";s:22:"Michael.rill@gmail.com";s:7:"subject";s:15:"[Michael Rill] ";s:12:"show_subject";s:2:"no";s:6:"widget";i:0;s:14:"block_template";N;s:19:"block_template_part";N;s:2:"id";i:2356;s:18:"submit_button_text";s:6:"Submit";s:14:"customThankyou";s:0:"";s:21:"customThankyouHeading";s:26:"Your message has been sent";s:21:"customThankyouMessage";s:30:"Thank you for your submission!";s:22:"customThankyouRedirect";s:0:"";s:10:"jetpackCRM";b:1;s:9:"className";N;s:9:"postToUrl";N;s:14:"salesforceData";N;s:12:"hiddenFields";N;}

item.title: Three things I appreciate
item.dc:creator: mcr
item.content:encoded:
Over the years, I’m becoming more aware of how I work and what I appreciate in others. As with most things in life these aren't revolutionary insights, but small observations that compound over time and make a big difference. They shape

These aren’t big revelations, but they’ve quietly shaped how I approach both my professional life and my personal one. I keep coming back to three ideas:

1. **Finishing Things You Start**: There’s something satisfying about seeing something through – not just kicking things off with a burst of energy, but following through to the end. Whether it's a project at work, a home repair, or a weekend plan, I find it frustrating when things are left hanging.

Of course, not everything we start needs to be finished at all costs. Priorities shift. But in general, making a habit of finishing builds trust – both with yourself and with others. It signals that you can be relied on, that your word means something, and that you're not just chasing the next shiny thing.

2. **Committing Fully (Not Half-Assing It):** If you're going to do something, do it properly. I’ve learned that halfway efforts often waste more time and energy in the long run. Whether it's a piece of work, a decision, or even a conversation, showing up fully matters.

This doesn’t mean perfectionism. It means being intentional. If I commit to something, I want to be all in – or not in at all. It’s a mindset that helps clarify priorities and leads to better outcomes, even in messy or ambiguous situations.

3. **Taking Care of Things**: Maintenance often doesn’t get much attention. It’s not glamorous. But taking care of what already exists – systems, tools, relationships – is often what makes everything else possible.

I’ve come to appreciate the people who quietly keep things running. The ones who patch the leak, update the documentation, follow up on the small detail that would otherwise slip. That work adds up. It creates stability. And in a world that often rewards starting over, I think it’s worth highlighting the value of sustaining what we already have.

These three values – finishing, committing, and maintaining – might not sound exciting, but they show up everywhere. They’re not about going above and beyond. They’re about showing up, consistently, and doing what needs to be done. I’ve found that when I keep them in mind, everything else tends to work a little better.

item.title: Be kind ... and other rules worth remembering
item.dc:creator: mcr
item.content:encoded:
I’ve always had a soft spot for the early 2000s TV show Scrubs. It’s smart, funny, and good at hiding profound reflections behind laughter. When the show moved to ABC for its eighth season, it suddenly shared a network with Sesame Street. They leaned into it with an episode featuring cameos from Sesame Street characters. The episode closed with a line that has stuck with me ever since:

"And then I realized why I thought about Sesame Street all day. In a way, you can learn everything you have to know from watching it as a kid. Like, always play nice… always try your hardest… and even, it’s okay to cry.”

Sixteen years later, I often think about that line. In fact, many of the lessons we learned as kids continue to be good advice later in life. A few others that come to mind:

Play well with others and take turns

In her recent docuseries Taylor Swift gave a great example of what that can look like: "I don’t want dancers that blend in. I don’t care about them pulling focus. I want them to pull focus. I want you to feel like you saw an entire crew of individual stars on the stage." I liked that a lot. Many workplaces still reward quiet conformity disguised as teamwork. I’ve seen wonderful things happen when people "let their freak flag fly" and lean into their uniqueness while also respecting the uniqueness of others. Suddenly you have a Voltron of the opposite work better: teams where people take turns being loud, visible, and opinionated, then step back without resentment. Real collaboration isn’t about shrinking yourself. It’s about trusting there will be space for you again.

Always try your hardest

"some people don't want to keep score, because they'll have to say, 'That's the best I can do.'" There is no shame in failing or losing as long as you can say "That's the best I can do."

Ask questions and admit when you don't know

Listen more than you speak

Clean up after yourself

Be kind

These ideas are simple, but they gain power in how consistently we practice them. I try to check in with myself every so often and ask: am I really living these out in how I work and how I show up for people?

The things that make us good colleagues – and good humans – are often the same lessons we absorbed before we could tie our shoes. Sometimes, the smartest move is to go back to those basics.
